quality.rationale: Combines relativistic tensor construction for arbitrary spin with thermal velocity averaging to extract field properties from macroscopic observables.

origin.question: Problem 12.15. New particles of mass $\mathfrak{m}_0$ are created which carry a new kind of charge, evidenced by a classical inverse-square force law. A container holds a perfect monatomic gas of these particles in thermal equilibrium. The total charge $Q$ in the container is measured by the average force on test particles outside, and it is observed to vary with temperature as $Q \propto Q_0(1 + 6kT / m_0)$ . What is the spin of the new force field?
origin.solution:
Solution 12.15. The geometrical object which tells about the charge is a scalar, vector, 2-tensor, etc. for spins 0, 1, 2, etc. For example, the charge density of a particle of rest-charge $\mathbf{q}_0$ moving along a worldline $\mathbf{x}^{\mu} = \mathbf{z}^{\mu}(\tau)$ with 4-velocity $\mathbf{u}$ is
$$
\rho \left(\mathbf {x}^{\sigma}\right) = q_{0} \int \delta^{4} \left[\mathbf {x}^{\mu} - z^{\mu} (\tau) \right] d \tau \quad (s c a l a r)
$$
$$
J^{\mu} \left(\mathbf {x}^{\sigma}\right) = q_{0} \int u^{\mu} \delta^{4} \left[\mathbf {x}^{\sigma} - z^{\sigma} (r) \right] d r \tag {1}
$$
$$
\mathrm {T}^{\mu \nu} (\mathbf {x}^{\sigma}) = q_{0} \int \mathrm{u}^{\mu} \mathrm{u}^{\nu} \delta^{4} [\mathbf {x}^{\sigma} - \mathbf{z}^{\sigma} (\tau) ] \mathrm{d} \tau \quad \text {(s p i n 2)}
$$
$$
\mathrm {T}^{\mu \nu \dot s \rho} (\mathbf {x}^{\sigma}) = q_{0} \int u^{\mu} u^{\nu} \dot s u^{\rho} \delta^{4} [\mathbf {x}^{\sigma} - z^{\sigma} (\tau) ] d \tau . \quad (\text {s p i n} \mathbf {s})
$$
The charge of a particle in a laboratory volume, measured by the "Coulomb" part of its interaction in the lab is
$$
\begin{array}{l}
q = \int T^{0 0 \dot s 0} d^{3} x (labframe) \\
= (- 1)^{s} \int T^{\alpha \beta \dot s \gamma} u_{\alpha}^{L A B} u_{\beta}^{L A B} \dot s u_{\gamma}^{L A B} d^{3} x. (2) \\
\end{array}
$$
Substituting Equation (1) into Equation (2) gives the measured charge of a single particle
$$
\begin{array}{l}
q = q_{0} \int d^{3} x \int d \tau (- u \cdot u^{\mathrm {L A B}})^{s} \delta^{4} [ x^{\sigma} - z^{\sigma} (r) ] \\
= q_{0} \int d^{3} x d t \left(\frac {d \tau}{d t}\right) (- u \cdot u^{\mathrm {L A B}})^{s} \delta^{4} [ x^{\sigma} - z^{\sigma} (\tau) ]. \\
\end{array}
$$
Integrating out the delta function, and using $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{u}^{LAB} = -\gamma$ and $\mathrm{d}\tau / \mathrm{dt} = 1 / \gamma$ , we get $q = q_0 \gamma^{s-1} \approx q_0 \left(1 + \frac{s-1}{2} v^2\right)$ for $v << c$ . Now for a perfect gas we have $<v^2> = 3kT / m_0$ . So when we sum the charges of all the particles in the gas we get
$$
\mathrm {Q} = \sum_{\mathbf{n}} \mathbf{q} = \mathrm{n q}_{0} \left[ 1 + \frac{\mathbf{s} - 1}{2} \left(\frac {3 \mathrm{k T}}{\mathbf{m}_{0}}\right) \right],
$$
and from the temperature dependence given in the problem we now read off $\mathbf{s} = 5$ .
